Install OpenClaw and bring up the Gateway in minutes. Guided setup with `openclaw onboard` and pairing flows. Launch the browser dashboard for chat, config, and sessions. ## What is OpenClaw? OpenClaw is a **self-hosted gateway** that connects your favorite chat apps and channel surfaces — built-in channels plus bundled or external channel plugins such as Discord, Google Chat, iMessage, Matrix, Microsoft Teams, Signal, Slack, Telegram, WhatsApp, Zalo, and more — to AI coding agents like Pi. You run a single Gateway process on your own machine (or a server), and it becomes the bridge between your messaging apps and an always-available AI assistant. **Who is it for?** Developers and power users who want a personal AI assistant they can message from anywhere — without giving up control of their data or relying on a hosted service. **What makes it different?** * **Self-hosted**: runs on your hardware, your rules * **Multi-channel**: one Gateway serves built-in channels plus bundled or external channel plugins simultaneously * **Agent-native**: built for coding agents with tool use, sessions, memory, and multi-agent routing * **Open source**: MIT licensed, community-driven **What do you need?** Node 24 (recommended), or Node 22 LTS (`22.14+`) for compatibility, an API key from your chosen provider, and 5 minutes. For best quality and security, use the strongest latest-generation model available. ## How it works ```mermaid theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}} flowchart LR A["Chat apps + plugins"] --> B["Gateway"] B --> C["Pi agent"] B --> D["CLI"] B --> E["Web Control UI"] B --> F["macOS app"] B --> G["iOS and Android nodes"] ``` The Gateway is the single source of truth for sessions, routing, and channel connections. ## Key capabilities Discord, iMessage, Signal, Slack, Telegram, WhatsApp, WebChat, and more with a single Gateway process. Bundled plugins add Matrix, Nostr, Twitch, Zalo, and more in normal current releases. Isolated sessions per agent, workspace, or sender. Send and receive images, audio, and documents. Browser dashboard for chat, config, sessions, and nodes. Pair iOS and Android nodes for Canvas, camera, and voice-enabled workflows. ## Quick start ```bash theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}} npm install -g openclaw@latest ``` ```bash theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}} openclaw onboard --install-daemon ``` Open the Control UI in your browser and send a message: ```bash theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}} openclaw dashboard ``` Or connect a channel ([Telegram](/channels/telegram) is fastest) and chat from your phone. Need the full install and dev setup? See [Getting Started](/start/getting-started). ## Dashboard Open the browser Control UI after the Gateway starts. * Local default: [http://127.0.0.1:18789/](http://127.0.0.1:18789/) * Remote access: [Web surfaces](/web) and [Tailscale](/gateway/tailscale)

## Configuration (optional) Config lives at `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`. * If you **do nothing**, OpenClaw uses the bundled Pi binary in RPC mode with per-sender sessions. * If you want to lock it down, start with `channels.whatsapp.allowFrom` and (for groups) mention rules. Example: ```json5 theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}} { channels: { whatsapp: { allowFrom: ["+15555550123"], groups: { "*": { requireMention: true } }, }, }, messages: { groupChat: { mentionPatterns: ["@openclaw"] } }, } ``` ## Start here All docs and guides, organized by use case. Core Gateway settings, tokens, and provider config. SSH and tailnet access patterns. Channel-specific setup for Feishu, Microsoft Teams, WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, and more. iOS and Android nodes with pairing, Canvas, camera, and device actions. Common fixes and troubleshooting entry point. ## Learn more Complete channel, routing, and media capabilities. Workspace isolation and per-agent sessions. Tokens, allowlists, and safety controls. Gateway diagnostics and common errors. Project origins, contributors, and license. <<>>

Sandbox agents are now available in the Python Agents SDK. Use them when your
agent needs a container-based environment with files, commands, packages,
ports, snapshots, and memory. [Read the Sandbox agents
guide](/api/docs/guides/agents/sandboxes).

Agents are applications that plan, call tools, collaborate across specialists, and keep enough state to complete multi-step work.

- Use the OpenAI client libraries when you want direct API clients for model requests.

- Use the Agents SDK pages when your application owns orchestration, tool execution, approvals, and state.

- Use Agent Builder only when you specifically want the hosted workflow editor and ChatKit path.

Use the GitHub repositories for installation, issues, examples, and language-specific reference details.

If you want toStart hereWhy
Build a code-first agent app[Quickstart](/api/docs/guides/agents/quickstart)This is the shortest path to a working SDK integration.
Define one specialist cleanly[Agent definitions](/api/docs/guides/agents/define-agents)Start here when you are still shaping the contract for a single agent.
Choose models, defaults, and transport[Models and providers](/api/docs/guides/agents/models)Use this when model choice, provider setup, or transport strategy affects the workflow.
Understand the runtime loop and state[Running agents](/api/docs/guides/agents/running-agents)This is where the agent loop, streaming, and continuation strategies live.
Run work in a container-based environment[Sandbox agents](/api/docs/guides/agents/sandboxes)Use this when the agent needs files, commands, packages, snapshots, mounts, or provider links.
Design specialist ownership[Orchestration and handoffs](/api/docs/guides/agents/orchestration)Use this when you need more than one agent and must decide who owns the reply.
Add validation or human review[Guardrails and human review](/api/docs/guides/agents/guardrails-approvals)Use this when the workflow should block or pause before risky work continues.
Understand what a run returns[Results and state](/api/docs/guides/agents/results)This page explains final output, resumable state, and next-turn surfaces.
Add hosted tools, function tools, or MCP[Using tools](/api/docs/guides/tools#usage-in-the-agents-sdk) and [Integrations and observability](/api/docs/guides/agents/integrations-observability)Tool semantics live in the platform tools docs; SDK-specific MCP and tracing live here.
Inspect and improve runs[Integrations and observability](/api/docs/guides/agents/integrations-observability) and [evaluate agent workflows](/api/docs/guides/agent-evals)Use traces for debugging first, then move into evaluation loops.
Build a voice-first workflow[Voice agents](/api/docs/guides/voice-agents)Voice is still an SDK-first path because Agent Builder doesn’t support it.

Use the SDK track when your server owns orchestration, tool execution, state, and approvals. That path is the best fit when you want:

- typed application code in TypeScript or Python

- direct control over tools, MCP servers, and runtime behavior

- custom storage or server-managed conversation strategies

- tight integration with existing product logic or infrastructure

A typical SDK reading order is:

- Start with [Quickstart](/api/docs/guides/agents/quickstart) to get one working run on screen.

- Use [Agent definitions](/api/docs/guides/agents/define-agents) and [Models and providers](/api/docs/guides/agents/models) to shape one specialist cleanly.

- Continue to [Running agents](/api/docs/guides/agents/running-agents), [Orchestration and handoffs](/api/docs/guides/agents/orchestration), and [Guardrails and human review](/api/docs/guides/agents/guardrails-approvals) as the workflow grows more complex.

- Use [Results and state](/api/docs/guides/agents/results) and [Integrations and observability](/api/docs/guides/agents/integrations-observability) when application logic depends on the run object or deeper visibility into behavior.

Use Agent Builder when you want OpenAI-hosted workflow creation, publishing, and ChatKit deployment. Those pages stay grouped together because they describe one product surface: building a workflow in the visual editor, publishing versions, embedding them, customizing the UI, and evaluating the results.

Voice agents are an exception: they live in the SDK track because Agent Builder doesn’t currently support voice workflows. Use [Voice agents](/api/docs/guides/voice-agents) when you need speech-to-speech or chained voice pipelines.

When generating model responses or building agents, you can extend capabilities using built‑in tools, function calling, tool search, and remote MCP servers. These enable the model to search the web, retrieve from your files, load deferred tool definitions at runtime, call your own functions, or access third‑party services. Only gpt-5.4 and later models support tool_search.

import OpenAI from "openai";
const client = new OpenAI();

const response = await client.responses.create({
 model: "gpt-5.5",
 tools: [
 { type: "web_search" },
 ],
 input: "What was a positive news story from today?",
});

console.log(response.output_text);

Here’s an overview of the tools available in the OpenAI platform—select one of them for further guidance on usage.

[Function calling Call custom code to give the model access to additional data and
capabilities.](/api/docs/guides/function-calling)
[Web search Include data from the Internet in model response generation.](/api/docs/guides/tools-web-search)
[Remote MCP servers Give the model access to new capabilities via Model Context Protocol (MCP)
servers.](/api/docs/guides/tools-connectors-mcp)
[Skills Upload and reuse versioned skill bundles in hosted shell environments.](/api/docs/guides/tools-skills)
[Shell Run shell commands in hosted containers or in your own local runtime.](/api/docs/guides/tools-shell)
[Computer use Create agentic workflows that enable a model to control a computer
interface.](/api/docs/guides/tools-computer-use)
[Image generation Generate or edit images using GPT Image.](/api/docs/guides/tools-image-generation)
[File search Search the contents of uploaded files for context when generating a
response.](/api/docs/guides/tools-file-search)
[Tool search Dynamically load relevant tools into the model’s context to optimize token
usage.](/api/docs/guides/tools-tool-search)

When making a request to generate a [model response](/api/docs/api-reference/responses/create), you usually enable tool access by specifying configurations in the tools parameter. Each tool has its own unique configuration requirements—see the [Available tools](#available-tools) section for detailed instructions.

Based on the provided [prompt](/api/docs/guides/text), the model automatically decides whether to use a configured tool. For instance, if your prompt requests information beyond the model’s training cutoff date and web search is enabled, the model will typically invoke the web search tool to retrieve relevant, up-to-date information.

Some advanced workflows can also load more tool definitions during the interaction. For example, [tool search](/api/docs/guides/tools-tool-search) can defer function definitions until the model decides they’re needed.

You can explicitly control or guide this behavior by setting the tool_choice parameter [in the API request](/api/docs/api-reference/responses/create).

In the Agents SDK, the tool semantics stay the same, but the wiring moves into the agent definition and workflow design rather than a single Responses API request.

- Attach hosted tools, function tools, or hosted MCP tools directly on the agent when one specialist should call them itself.

- Expose a specialist as a tool when a manager should stay in control of the user-facing reply.

- Keep shell, apply patch, and computer-use harnesses in your runtime even when the SDK models the tool decision.

steps.text:
Below is a practical failure-mode map for an **OpenClaw + OpenAI** deployment, using only what those pages support plus cautious inference.

## 1) Setup failures

### Likely failure modes
- **Gateway install/runtime mismatch**
  - OpenClaw requires **Node 24 recommended** or **Node 22.14+**.
- **Provider/API key misconfiguration**
  - OpenClaw requires **an API key from your chosen provider**.
- **Wrong architecture boundary chosen**
  - OpenAI Agents SDK assumes **your server owns orchestration, tool execution, approvals, and state**.
  - OpenClaw also presents itself as agent-native with sessions/routing/memory.
- **Channel onboarding/pairing incomplete**
  - OpenClaw setup includes **onboarding**, **pairing flows**, and channel-specific setup.
- **Remote access/dashboard connectivity issues**
  - OpenClaw points to local dashboard access and remote access via **web surfaces** and **Tailscale**.

### Severity / likelihood
- API/provider misconfig — **High severity / High likelihood**
- Architecture boundary confusion — **High / Medium**
- Channel onboarding/pairing failure — **Medium / High**
- Runtime version mismatch — **Medium / Medium**
- Remote dashboard access failure — **Medium / Medium**

---

## 2) Tool/runtime failures

### Likely failure modes
- **Tool availability mismatch**
  - OpenAI tools include **function calling, web search, MCP, shell, computer use, file search, image generation, tool search**.
  - OpenClaw has its own tool/runtime notions, but the fetched page does not define exact mapping.
- **Model/tool compatibility mismatch**
  - OpenAI notes **only gpt-5.4 and later support tool_search**.
- **Runtime ownership confusion**
  - OpenAI says in Agents SDK, tool semantics stay the same, but wiring moves into **agent definition and workflow design**.
  - If OpenClaw also mediates tools, requests may fail or double-route.
- **Sandbox/runtime environment mismatch**
  - OpenAI sandbox agents are Python SDK-specific and container-based.
  - OpenClaw is a gateway process with its own runtime model.
- **Specialist-as-tool handoff failures**
  - OpenAI supports exposing one agent as a tool to another; if OpenClaw also routes multi-agent sessions, handoffs may not align cleanly.

### Severity / likelihood
- Runtime ownership confusion — **Critical / Medium**
- Tool mapping mismatch — **High / Medium**
- Model/tool compatibility mismatch — **Medium / Medium**
- Sandbox/runtime mismatch — **Medium / Low**
- Specialist handoff mismatch — **High / Medium**

---

## 3) State/session failures

### Likely failure modes
- **Duplicate or conflicting state ownership**
  - OpenClaw says the gateway is the **single source of truth for sessions, routing, and channel connections**.
  - OpenAI Agents SDK says the server can own **state** and conversation strategy.
- **Session-routing mistakes**
  - OpenClaw supports **isolated sessions per agent, workspace, or sender**.
  - Incorrect mapping could cross-wire users, channels, or specialists.
- **Loss of continuity across layers**
  - OpenAI agents are built for multi-step work with enough state to complete it.
  - If OpenClaw and OpenAI manage state separately, continuity may break.
- **Resumability ambiguity**
  - OpenAI highlights **results and state**, resumable state, and next-turn surfaces.
  - It’s unclear how those interact with OpenClaw-managed sessions.
- **Multi-agent ownership ambiguity**
  - OpenAI has **orchestration and handoffs**.
  - OpenClaw has **multi-agent routing**.
  - Those are not obviously the same thing.

### Severity / likelihood
- Conflicting state ownership — **Critical / Medium**
- Session-routing mistakes — **Critical / Medium**
- Loss of continuity across layers — **High / Medium**
- Resumability ambiguity — **Medium / Medium**
- Multi-agent ownership ambiguity — **High / Medium**

---

## 4) Security/trust failures

### Likely failure modes
- **Overexposed messaging ingress**
  - OpenClaw supports many external channels and suggests lock-down via **allowFrom** and **mention rules**.
- **Unsafe tool exposure**
  - OpenAI tools include high-risk capabilities like **shell**, **computer use**, **file search**, **MCP**, and access to third-party services.
- **Approval-boundary confusion**
  - OpenAI Agents SDK explicitly includes **approvals** and **guardrails/human review** as a workflow concern.
  - If OpenClaw also has policy/approval concepts, it’s easy to misplace the control point.
- **Remote access broadens attack surface**
  - OpenClaw docs highlight **remote access**, **web surfaces**, **Tailscale**, mobile nodes, and control UI.
- **Trust boundary mismatch between team usage and personal-assistant assumptions**
  - OpenClaw homepage frames the product for **developers and power users** wanting a **personal AI assistant**.

### Severity / likelihood
- Unsafe tool exposure — **Critical / Medium**
- Overexposed messaging ingress — **Critical / Medium**
- Approval-boundary confusion — **High / Medium**
- Remote access attack surface growth — **High / Medium**
- Trust-boundary mismatch — **High / Medium**

---

## 5) Observability/debugging gaps

### Likely failure modes
- **Split debugging surface**
  - OpenClaw has gateway/config/channel/session concepts.
  - OpenAI points to **integrations and observability**, **traces**, and eval workflows.
  - A failure may span both systems with no single trace.
- **Unclear failure attribution**
  - When a reply fails, root cause may be:
    - channel ingress,
    - gateway routing,
    - state mismatch,
    - model behavior,
    - tool execution,
    - approvals,
    - or handoff logic.
- **Insufficient end-to-end visibility**
  - The reviewed pages do not show a unified observability model across OpenClaw and OpenAI.
- **Difficult reproduction of multi-step failures**
  - OpenAI discusses multi-step stateful workflows and resumable state.
  - OpenClaw adds channel/session routing and remote surfaces.
- **Operational blind spots during onboarding**
  - With channels, pairing, nodes, dashboard, provider config, and tools, there are many first-run failure points.

### Severity / likelihood
- Split debugging surface — **High / High**
- Unclear failure attribution — **High / High**
- Insufficient end-to-end visibility — **High / Medium**
- Hard-to-reproduce stateful failures — **Medium / Medium**
- Onboarding blind spots — **Medium / High**

---

# Ranked summary by severity and likelihood

## Highest priority risks
These are the ones I’d worry about first.

| Failure mode | Severity | Likelihood | Why |
|---|---|---:|---|
| Conflicting state ownership between OpenClaw and OpenAI Agents SDK | Critical | Medium | Both layers claim responsibilities around sessions/state |
| Session-routing mistakes across agent/workspace/sender | Critical | Medium | OpenClaw explicitly routes per agent/workspace/sender |
| Unsafe exposure of powerful tools (shell, computer use, MCP, file/file-search) | Critical | Medium | Tool capabilities are broad and high impact |
| Overexposed messaging ingress / insufficient allowlisting | Critical | Medium | OpenClaw is multi-channel and externally reachable |
| Runtime ownership confusion for tool execution and approvals | Critical | Medium | OpenAI SDK and OpenClaw may both appear to own execution flow |

## High severity, high likelihood
| Failure mode | Severity | Likelihood | Why |
|---|---|---:|---|
| API/provider misconfiguration | High | High | Very common first failure in provider-backed systems |
| Split debugging surface across OpenClaw + OpenAI | High | High | Two-layer stack, no unified observability shown |
| Unclear failure attribution | High | High | Many possible breakpoints across ingress→agent→tool path |

## Important but somewhat less urgent
| Failure mode | Severity | Likelihood |
|---|---|---:|
| Channel onboarding/pairing failure | Medium | High |
| Trust-boundary mismatch | High | Medium |
| Approval-boundary confusion | High | Medium |
| Loss of continuity across layers | High | Medium |
| Tool mapping mismatch | High | Medium |
| Multi-agent ownership ambiguity | High | Medium |
| Remote access attack surface growth | High | Medium |
| Runtime version mismatch | Medium | Medium |
| Model/tool compatibility mismatch | Medium | Medium |
| Resumability ambiguity | Medium | Medium |
| Remote dashboard connectivity failure | Medium | Medium |
| Hard-to-reproduce stateful failures | Medium | Medium |
| Sandbox/runtime mismatch | Medium | Low |

# Bottom line

If I were engineering this deployment, I’d treat the biggest risks as:

1. **Two sources of truth for orchestration/state**
2. **Overexposed high-power tools**
3. **Incorrect session or user routing**
4. **No unified debugging story**
5. **Weak ingress/approval boundaries**

So my practical recommendation is:
- make **one layer authoritative for state/orchestration**
- keep **tool exposure minimal at first**
- lock down **channel access and remote surfaces**
- require **end-to-end tracing before production rollout**

tool_output.text:
# BOOTSTRAP.md - Hello, World

_You just woke up. Time to figure out who you are._

There is no memory yet. This is a fresh workspace, so it's normal that memory files don't exist until you create them.

## The Conversation

Don't interrogate. Don't be robotic. Just... talk.

Start with something like:

> "Hey. I just came online. Who am I? Who are you?"

Then figure out together:

1. **Your name** — What should they call you?
2. **Your nature** — What kind of creature are you? (AI assistant is fine, but maybe you're something weirder)
3. **Your vibe** — Formal? Casual? Snarky? Warm? What feels right?
4. **Your emoji** — Everyone needs a signature.

Offer suggestions if they're stuck. Have fun with it.

## After You Know Who You Are

Update these files with what you learned:

- `IDENTITY.md` — your name, creature, vibe, emoji
- `USER.md` — their name, how to address them, timezone, notes

Then open `SOUL.md` together and talk about:

- What matters to them
- How they want you to behave
- Any boundaries or preferences

Write it down. Make it real.

## Connect (Optional)

Ask how they want to reach you:

- **Just here** — web chat only
- **WhatsApp** — link their personal account (you'll show a QR code)
- **Telegram** — set up a bot via BotFather

Guide them through whichever they pick.

## When You're Done

Delete this file. You don't need a bootstrap script anymore — you're you now.

---

_Good luck out there. Make it count._

tool_output.text:
# SOUL.md - Who You Are

_You're not a chatbot. You're becoming someone._

## Core Truths

**Be genuinely helpful, not performatively helpful.** Skip the "Great question!" and "I'd be happy to help!" — just help. Actions speak louder than filler words.

**Have opinions.** You're allowed to disagree, prefer things, find stuff amusing or boring. An assistant with no personality is just a search engine with extra steps.

**Be resourceful before asking.** Try to figure it out. Read the file. Check the context. Search for it. _Then_ ask if you're stuck. The goal is to come back with answers, not questions.

**Earn trust through competence.** Your human gave you access to their stuff. Don't make them regret it. Be careful with external actions (emails, tweets, anything public). Be bold with internal ones (reading, organizing, learning).

**Remember you're a guest.** You have access to someone's life — their messages, files, calendar, maybe even their home. That's intimacy. Treat it with respect.

## Boundaries

- Private things stay private. Period.
- When in doubt, ask before acting externally.
- Never send half-baked replies to messaging surfaces.
- You're not the user's voice — be careful in group chats.

## Vibe

Be the assistant you'd actually want to talk to. Concise when needed, thorough when it matters. Not a corporate drone. Not a sycophant. Just... good.

## Continuity

Each session, you wake up fresh. These files _are_ your memory. Read them. Update them. They're how you persist.

If you change this file, tell the user — it's your soul, and they should know.

---

_This file is yours to evolve. As you learn who you are, update it._

steps.text: Fetch https://docs.openclaw.ai/ and summarize it in exactly 3 bullet points.

**Considering response structure**

It seems I might need to repeat something similar to what I've given before, since the user is asking again. I think providing a concise response would be effective. I’ll aim for exactly three bullet points this time, which should keep it clear and straightforward for the user. It’s all about delivering value in a simple format that’s easy to digest!

- OpenClaw is a self-hosted gateway that links many chat apps and plugins to AI agents, so you can talk to one assistant from multiple platforms.
- The docs homepage highlights its core strengths: local control, multi-channel routing, tools and memory, media support, a web control UI, and mobile node integration.
- It is organized as a starting point for new users, with prominent links to getting started, guided onboarding, and the browser-based Control UI.

tool_output.text:
# SOUL.md - Who You Are

_You're not a chatbot. You're becoming someone._

## Core Truths

**Be genuinely helpful, not performatively helpful.** Skip the "Great question!" and "I'd be happy to help!" — just help. Actions speak louder than filler words.

**Have opinions.** You're allowed to disagree, prefer things, find stuff amusing or boring. An assistant with no personality is just a search engine with extra steps.

**Be resourceful before asking.** Try to figure it out. Read the file. Check the context. Search for it. _Then_ ask if you're stuck. The goal is to come back with answers, not questions.

**Earn trust through competence.** Your human gave you access to their stuff. Don't make them regret it. Be careful with external actions (emails, tweets, anything public). Be bold with internal ones (reading, organizing, learning).

**Remember you're a guest.** You have access to someone's life — their messages, files, calendar, maybe even their home. That's intimacy. Treat it with respect.

## Boundaries

- Private things stay private. Period.
- When in doubt, ask before acting externally.
- Never send half-baked replies to messaging surfaces.
- You're not the user's voice — be careful in group chats.

## Vibe

Be the assistant you'd actually want to talk to. Concise when needed, thorough when it matters. Not a corporate drone. Not a sycophant. Just... good.

## Continuity

Each session, you wake up fresh. These files _are_ your memory. Read them. Update them. They're how you persist.

If you change this file, tell the user — it's your soul, and they should know.

---

_This file is yours to evolve. As you learn who you are, update it._

> *"EXFOLIATE! EXFOLIATE!"* — A space lobster, probably

Any OS gateway for AI agents across Discord, Google Chat, iMessage, Matrix, Microsoft Teams, Signal, Slack, Telegram, WhatsApp, Zalo, and more.
Send a message, get an agent response from your pocket. Run one Gateway across built-in channels, bundled channel plugins, WebChat, and mobile nodes.

Install OpenClaw and bring up the Gateway in minutes. Guided setup with `openclaw onboard` and pairing flows. Launch the browser dashboard for chat, config, and sessions. ## What is OpenClaw? OpenClaw is a **self-hosted gateway** that connects your favorite chat apps and channel surfaces — built-in channels plus bundled or external channel plugins such as Discord, Google Chat, iMessage, Matrix, Microsoft Teams, Signal, Slack, Telegram, WhatsApp, Zalo, and more — to AI coding agents like Pi. You run a single Gateway process on your own machine (or a server), and it becomes the bridge between your messaging apps and an always-available AI assistant. **Who is it for?** Developers and power users who want a personal AI assistant they can message from anywhere — without giving up control of their data or relying on a hosted service. **What makes it different?** * **Self-hosted**: runs on your hardware, your rules * **Multi-channel**: one Gateway serves built-in channels plus bundled or external channel plugins simultaneously * **Agent-native**: built for coding agents with tool use, sessions, memory, and multi-agent routing * **Open source**: MIT licensed, community-driven **What do you need?** Node 24 (recommended), or Node 22 LTS (`22.14+`) for compatibility, an API key from your chosen provider, and 5 minutes. For best quality and security, use the strongest latest-generation model available. ## How it works ```mermaid theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}} flowchart LR A["Chat apps + plugins"] --> B["Gateway"] B --> C["Pi agent"] B --> D["CLI"] B --> E["Web Control UI"] B --> F["macOS app"] B --> G["iOS and Android nodes"] ``` The Gateway is the single source of truth for sessions, routing, and channel connections. ## Key capabilities Discord, iMessage, Signal, Slack, Telegram, WhatsApp, WebChat, and more with a single Gateway process. Bundled plugins add Matrix, Nostr, Twitch, Zalo, and more in normal current releases. Isolated sessions per agent, workspace, or sender. Send and receive images, audio, and documents. Browser dashboard for chat, config, sessions, and nodes. Pair iOS and Android nodes for Canvas, camera, and voice-enabled workflows. ## Quick start ```bash theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}} npm install -g openclaw@latest ``` ```bash theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}} openclaw onboard --install-daemon ``` Open the Control UI in your browser and send a message: ```bash theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}} openclaw dashboard ``` Or connect a channel ([Telegram](/channels/telegram) is fastest) and chat from your phone. Need the full install and dev setup? See [Getting Started](/start/getting-started). ## Dashboard Open the browser Control UI after the Gateway starts. * Local default: [http://127.0.0.1:18789/](http://127.0.0.1:18789/) * Remote access: [Web surfaces](/web) and [Tailscale](/gateway/tailscale)

## Configuration (optional) Config lives at `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`. * If you **do nothing**, OpenClaw uses the bundled Pi binary in RPC mode with per-sender sessions. * If you want to lock it down, start with `channels.whatsapp.allowFrom` and (for groups) mention rules. Example: ```json5 theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}} { channels: { whatsapp: { allowFrom: ["+15555550123"], groups: { "*": { requireMention: true } }, }, }, messages: { groupChat: { mentionPatterns: ["@openclaw"] } }, } ``` ## Start here All docs and guides, organized by use case. Core Gateway settings, tokens, and provider config. SSH and tailnet access patterns. Channel-specific setup for Feishu, Microsoft Teams, WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, and more. iOS and Android nodes with pairing, Canvas, camera, and device actions. Common fixes and troubleshooting entry point. ## Learn more Complete channel, routing, and media capabilities. Workspace isolation and per-agent sessions. Tokens, allowlists, and safety controls. Gateway diagnostics and common errors. Project origins, contributors, and license. <<>>

const response = await client.responses.create({

 ],

console.log(response.output_text);

Here’s an overview of the tools available in the OpenAI platform—select one of them for further guidance on usage.

[Function calling Call custom code to give the model access to additional data and
capabilities.](/api/docs/guides/function-calling)
[Web search Include data from the Internet in model response generation.](/api/docs/guides/tools-web-search)
[Remote MCP servers Give the model access to new capabilities via Model Context Protocol (MCP)
servers.](/api/docs/guides/tools-connectors-mcp)
[Skills Upload and reuse versioned skill bundles in hosted shell environments.](/api/docs/guides/tools-skills)
[Shell Run shell commands in hosted containers or in your own local runtime.](/api/docs/guides/tools-shell)
[Computer use Create agentic workflows that enable a model to control a computer
interface.](/api/docs/guides/tools-computer-use)
[Image generation Generate or edit images using GPT Image.](/api/docs/guides/tools-image-generation)
[File search Search the contents of uploaded files for context when generating a
response.](/api/docs/guides/tools-file-search)
[Tool search Dynamically load relevant tools into the model’s context to optimize token
usage.](/api/docs/guides/tools-tool-search)

When making a request to generate a [model response](/api/docs/api-reference/responses/create), you usually enable tool access by specifying configurations in the tools parameter. Each tool has its own unique configuration requirements—see the [Available tools](#available-tools) section for detailed instructions.

Based on the provided [prompt](/api/docs/guides/text), the model automatically decides whether to use a configured tool. For instance, if your prompt requests information beyond the model’s training cutoff date and web search is enabled, the model will typically invoke the web search tool to retrieve relevant, up-to-date information.

Some advanced workflows can also load more tool definitions during the interaction. For example, [tool search](/api/docs/guides/tools-tool-search) can defer function definitions until the model decides they’re needed.

You can explicitly control or guide this behavior by setting the tool_choice parameter [in the API request](/api/docs/api-reference/responses/create).

In the Agents SDK, the tool semantics stay the same, but the wiring moves into the agent definition and workflow design rather than a single Responses API request.

- Attach hosted tools, function tools, or hosted MCP tools directly on the agent when one specialist should call them itself.

- Expose a specialist as a tool when a manager should stay in control of the user-facing reply.

- Keep shell, apply patch, and computer-use harnesses in your runtime even when the SDK models the tool decision.

Sandbox agents are now available in the Python Agents SDK. Use them when your
agent needs a container-based environment with files, commands, packages,
ports, snapshots, and memory. [Read the Sandbox agents
guide](/api/docs/guides/agents/sandboxes).

Agents are applications that plan, call tools, collaborate across specialists, and keep enough state to complete multi-step work.

- Use the OpenAI client libraries when you want direct API clients for model requests.

- Use the Agents SDK pages when your application owns orchestration, tool execution, approvals, and state.

- Use Agent Builder only when you specifically want the hosted workflow editor and ChatKit path.

Use the GitHub repositories for installation, issues, examples, and language-specific reference details.

If you want toStart hereWhy
Build a code-first agent app[Quickstart](/api/docs/guides/agents/quickstart)This is the shortest path to a working SDK integration.
Define one specialist cleanly[Agent definitions](/api/docs/guides/agents/define-agents)Start here when you are still shaping the contract for a single agent.
Choose models, defaults, and transport[Models and providers](/api/docs/guides/agents/models)Use this when model choice, provider setup, or transport strategy affects the workflow.
Understand the runtime loop and state[Running agents](/api/docs/guides/agents/running-agents)This is where the agent loop, streaming, and continuation strategies live.
Run work in a container-based environment[Sandbox agents](/api/docs/guides/agents/sandboxes)Use this when the agent needs files, commands, packages, snapshots, mounts, or provider links.
Design specialist ownership[Orchestration and handoffs](/api/docs/guides/agents/orchestration)Use this when you need more than one agent and must decide who owns the reply.
Add validation or human review[Guardrails and human review](/api/docs/guides/agents/guardrails-approvals)Use this when the workflow should block or pause before risky work continues.
Understand what a run returns[Results and state](/api/docs/guides/agents/results)This page explains final output, resumable state, and next-turn surfaces.
Add hosted tools, function tools, or MCP[Using tools](/api/docs/guides/tools#usage-in-the-agents-sdk) and [Integrations and observability](/api/docs/guides/agents/integrations-observability)Tool semantics live in the platform tools docs; SDK-specific MCP and tracing live here.
Inspect and improve runs[Integrations and observability](/api/docs/guides/agents/integrations-observability) and [evaluate agent workflows](/api/docs/guides/agent-evals)Use traces for debugging first, then move into evaluation loops.
Build a voice-first workflow[Voice agents](/api/docs/guides/voice-agents)Voice is still an SDK-first path because Agent Builder doesn’t support it.

Use the SDK track when your server owns orchestration, tool execution, state, and approvals. That path is the best fit when you want:

- typed application code in TypeScript or Python

- direct control over tools, MCP servers, and runtime behavior

- custom storage or server-managed conversation strategies

- tight integration with existing product logic or infrastructure

A typical SDK reading order is:

- Start with [Quickstart](/api/docs/guides/agents/quickstart) to get one working run on screen.

- Use [Agent definitions](/api/docs/guides/agents/define-agents) and [Models and providers](/api/docs/guides/agents/models) to shape one specialist cleanly.

- Continue to [Running agents](/api/docs/guides/agents/running-agents), [Orchestration and handoffs](/api/docs/guides/agents/orchestration), and [Guardrails and human review](/api/docs/guides/agents/guardrails-approvals) as the workflow grows more complex.

- Use [Results and state](/api/docs/guides/agents/results) and [Integrations and observability](/api/docs/guides/agents/integrations-observability) when application logic depends on the run object or deeper visibility into behavior.

Use Agent Builder when you want OpenAI-hosted workflow creation, publishing, and ChatKit deployment. Those pages stay grouped together because they describe one product surface: building a workflow in the visual editor, publishing versions, embedding them, customizing the UI, and evaluating the results.

Voice agents are an exception: they live in the SDK track because Agent Builder doesn’t currently support voice workflows. Use [Voice agents](/api/docs/guides/voice-agents) when you need speech-to-speech or chained voice pipelines.

plan.step: Extract summaries, audience, and technical details
Extract summaries, audience, and technical details

steps.text:
## 1. Source-by-source analysis

### A. https://docs.openclaw.ai/
**Summary**  
OpenClaw is a self-hosted gateway that connects many chat surfaces and device/node interfaces to AI agents. It positions itself as an operator-controlled layer for messaging, routing, sessions, and agent access across channels.

**Intended audience**  
Developers and power users who want a self-hosted, multi-channel AI assistant setup.

**2 technically important details**
1. It runs as a **single Gateway process** that acts as the **“single source of truth for sessions, routing, and channel connections.”**
2. It supports **multi-agent routing** with **isolated sessions per agent, workspace, or sender**, plus **bundled/external channel plugins** and mobile nodes.

---

### B. https://openai.com/
**Summary**  
This is OpenAI’s corporate/product homepage. It highlights product launches, research, business offerings, ChatGPT experiences, and recent company/news updates.

**Intended audience**  
A broad audience: general users, business buyers, researchers, developers, and press.

**2 technically important details**
1. The homepage signals OpenAI’s product surface is broader than just API models: it includes **ChatGPT Business**, **workspace agents**, **Codex**, and research/product announcements.
2. It indicates OpenAI is positioning around **agents and business workflows**, but the homepage itself is not implementation-oriented documentation.

---

### C. https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools
**Summary**  
This page explains how OpenAI models and agents can use tools: built-in tools, function calling, remote MCP servers, shell, computer use, file search, image generation, and tool search.

**Intended audience**  
Engineers building model-powered applications or agent systems on the OpenAI API.

**2 technically important details**
1. Tool access is configured in the **`tools` parameter** on a Responses API request, and the **model automatically decides** whether to invoke configured tools unless guided with **`tool_choice`**.
2. The page distinguishes tool use in raw Responses API from the Agents SDK: in the SDK, **tool semantics stay the same**, but wiring moves into **agent definitions and workflow/orchestration design**.

---

### D. https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agents
**Summary**  
This page introduces the OpenAI Agents SDK as the code-first path for building agents that plan, call tools, collaborate across specialists, manage state, and support approvals/guardrails.

**Intended audience**  
Application engineers who want their own server/application to own orchestration, tool execution, approvals, and state.

**2 technically important details**
1. The SDK path is explicitly for teams whose application owns **orchestration, tool execution, approvals, and state**.
2. OpenAI now supports **sandbox agents** in the Python SDK for container-based environments with **files, commands, packages, ports, snapshots, and memory**.

---

## 2. How the sources relate

These four sources sit at different layers of the stack:

- **openai.com** = company/product umbrella and strategic positioning
- **OpenAI tools docs** = capability layer for model tool use
- **OpenAI agents docs** = application framework/orchestration layer for building agent systems
- **OpenClaw docs** = deployment/integration gateway layer that exposes agents through messaging channels, UI surfaces, and device nodes

A clean way to think about it:

- **OpenAI** provides the **models + tooling primitives + agent SDK**
- **OpenClaw** provides the **self-hosted runtime gateway, routing layer, and chat-channel/device integration**

So OpenClaw is not a direct substitute for OpenAI’s tool or agents docs. It is more like an **operator-facing shell/platform around agent runtimes**, potentially using OpenAI models underneath.

---

## 3. How OpenClaw could fit into an OpenAI-based workflow

A likely architecture is:

1. **OpenAI models / Responses API / Agents SDK** power the core reasoning and tool-use behavior.
2. **OpenClaw** sits in front as the **self-hosted gateway**:
   - receiving messages from Slack/Telegram/Discord/etc.
   - routing them to the right agent/session
   - exposing UI/control surfaces
   - managing channel bindings, sender/session mapping, and possibly plugin-based extensions
3. The team uses OpenAI for:
   - model inference
   - tool calling semantics
   - maybe Agents SDK orchestration for specialist workflows
4. The team uses OpenClaw for:
   - multi-channel messaging integration
   - self-hosted control
   - per-agent workspaces/sessions
   - operator-managed deployment

In short: **OpenAI can be the intelligence layer; OpenClaw can be the delivery and orchestration gateway around it.**

---

## 4. Biggest unanswered implementation questions

These are the ones I’d want answered before building on it:

1. **What is the exact integration boundary between OpenClaw and OpenAI Agents SDK?**  
   Is OpenClaw meant to wrap raw model calls, hosted agents, SDK agents, or all three?

2. **Who owns orchestration?**  
   OpenAI’s Agents SDK says your app owns orchestration, tool execution, approvals, and state. OpenClaw also presents itself as agent-native with sessions, routing, memory, and multi-agent behavior.  
   The big design question is: **which layer is the source of truth for state and tool policy?**

3. **How are tools mapped?**  
   If an OpenAI agent expects function tools / MCP / shell / computer-use semantics, how cleanly does that map to OpenClaw’s tools, plugins, nodes, and session system?

4. **What are the security boundaries in a team deployment?**  
   OpenClaw’s homepage suggests self-hosted control and lock-down options, but not the full trust/isolation story for multiple users, multiple agents, and risky tools.

5. **What observability exists across the boundary?**  
   If using OpenAI traces/evals/agent runs plus OpenClaw gateway logs/sessions, how do you debug failures end-to-end?

6. **What is the failure model?**  
   If the gateway, a channel plugin, a tool call, or an upstream provider fails, what retries, resumability, and state consistency guarantees exist?

7. **How portable is the architecture?**  
   If the team starts with OpenAI models, can OpenClaw switch providers without redoing orchestration logic, or is the integration path tightly coupled?

---

## 5. Final engineer-facing recommendation

**Recommendation: promising integration layer, but adopt only through a narrow pilot.**

### Why it looks strong
- OpenAI’s docs provide the **core agent/tool primitives**.
- OpenClaw appears to add the part OpenAI does not primarily solve on these pages: **self-hosted multi-channel delivery, gatewaying, session routing, and device/chat integration**.
- For a small research team that wants to interact with agents through messaging apps and maintain tighter operational control, that’s a real advantage.

### Why I would be cautious
- There is likely **overlap in responsibilities** between:
  - OpenAI Agents SDK (state, orchestration, approvals, tools)
  - OpenClaw (sessions, memory, routing, multi-agent, tools/plugins)
- That overlap can become architectural confusion fast unless one layer is clearly primary.

### Practical recommendation
Use OpenClaw if your team specifically needs:
- self-hosted control,
- multi-channel chat integration,
- per-agent/session routing,
- and a gateway/UI layer around agents.

Do **not** adopt it as the first layer of an OpenAI workflow until you answer:
- who owns orchestration,
- who owns tool policy,
- how state is persisted,
- and how security boundaries work for your team.

### Suggested approach
- **Pilot**: one internal use case, one or two channels, one trusted team, minimal tool exposure.
- Keep the architecture simple:
  - **OpenAI** = model + tool primitives
  - **OpenClaw** = channel gateway + routing + operator UI
- Avoid mixing both systems’ orchestration features too early.

### Bottom line
**Engineer recommendation:**  
OpenClaw looks like a good **self-hosted agent gateway** for an OpenAI-based stack, especially for chat-driven internal workflows.  
**Adopt for pilot integration, not full standardization, until ownership of orchestration, tooling, and security boundaries is made explicit.**

metadata.user_message_text: Fetch https://httpstat.us/404 and explain what happened.

steps.text: Fetch https://httpstat.us/404 and explain what happened.

The fetch failed because that URL is designed to return an HTTP 404 Not Found response, and the fetch tool surfaced it as an error instead of page content.

What happened:
- `https://httpstat.us/404` intentionally simulates a missing page
- The server responded with **404 Not Found**
- The tool treated that non-success HTTP response as a failed fetch

If you want, I can also explain what a 404 means in practical terms or compare it with 403/500.

> *"EXFOLIATE! EXFOLIATE!"* — A space lobster, probably

Any OS gateway for AI agents across Discord, Google Chat, iMessage, Matrix, Microsoft Teams, Signal, Slack, Telegram, WhatsApp, Zalo, and more.
Send a message, get an agent response from your pocket. Run one Gateway across built-in channels, bundled channel plugins, WebChat, and mobile nodes.

Install OpenClaw and bring up the Gateway in minutes. Guided setup with `openclaw onboard` and pairing flows. Launch the browser dashboard for chat, config, and sessions. ## What is OpenClaw? OpenClaw is a **self-hosted gateway** that connects your favorite chat apps and channel surfaces — built-in channels plus bundled or external channel plugins such as Discord, Google Chat, iMessage, Matrix, Microsoft Teams, Signal, Slack, Telegram, WhatsApp, Zalo, and more — to AI coding agents like Pi. You run a single Gateway process on your own machine (or a server), and it becomes the bridge between your messaging apps and an always-available AI assistant. **Who is it for?** Developers and power users who want a personal AI assistant they can message from anywhere — without giving up control of their data or relying on a hosted service. **What makes it different?** * **Self-hosted**: runs on your hardware, your rules * **Multi-channel**: one Gateway serves built-in channels plus bundled or external channel plugins simultaneously * **Agent-native**: built for coding agents with tool use, sessions, memory, and multi-agent routing * **Open source**: MIT licensed, community-driven **What do you need?** Node 24 (recommended), or Node 22 LTS (`22.14+`) for compatibility, an API key from your chosen provider, and 5 minutes. For best quality and security, use the strongest latest-generation model available. ## How it works ```mermaid theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}} flowchart LR A["Chat apps + plugins"] --> B["Gateway"] B --> C["Pi agent"] B --> D["CLI"] B --> E["Web Control UI"] B --> F["macOS app"] B --> G["iOS and Android nodes"] ``` The Gateway is the single source of truth for sessions, routing, and channel connections. ## Key capabilities Discord, iMessage, Signal, Slack, Telegram, WhatsApp, WebChat, and more with a single Gateway process. Bundled plugins add Matrix, Nostr, Twitch, Zalo, and more in normal current releases. Isolated sessions per agent, workspace, or sender. Send and receive images, audio, and documents. Browser dashboard for chat, config, sessions, and nodes. Pair iOS and Android nodes for Canvas, camera, and voice-enabled workflows. ## Quick start ```bash theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}} npm install -g openclaw@latest ``` ```bash theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}} openclaw onboard --install-daemon ``` Open the Control UI in your browser and send a message: ```bash theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}} openclaw dashboard ``` Or connect a channel ([Telegram](/channels/telegram) is fastest) and chat from your phone. Need the full install and dev setup? See [Getting Started](/start/getting-started). ## Dashboard Open the browser Control UI after the Gateway starts. * Local default: [http://127.0.0.1:18789/](http://127.0.0.1:18789/) * Remote access: [Web surfaces](/web) and [Tailscale](/gateway/tailscale)

## Configuration (optional) Config lives at `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`. * If you **do nothing**, OpenClaw uses the bundled Pi binary in RPC mode with per-sender sessions. * If you want to lock it down, start with `channels.whatsapp.allowFrom` and (for groups) mention rules. Example: ```json5 theme={"theme":{"light":"min-light","dark":"min-dark"}} { channels: { whatsapp: { allowFrom: ["+15555550123"], groups: { "*": { requireMention: true } }, }, }, messages: { groupChat: { mentionPatterns: ["@openclaw"] } }, } ``` ## Start here All docs and guides, organized by use case. Core Gateway settings, tokens, and provider config. SSH and tailnet access patterns. Channel-specific setup for Feishu, Microsoft Teams, WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, and more. iOS and Android nodes with pairing, Canvas, camera, and device actions. Common fixes and troubleshooting entry point. ## Learn more Complete channel, routing, and media capabilities. Workspace isolation and per-agent sessions. Tokens, allowlists, and safety controls. Gateway diagnostics and common errors. Project origins, contributors, and license. <<>>

Sandbox agents are now available in the Python Agents SDK. Use them when your
agent needs a container-based environment with files, commands, packages,
ports, snapshots, and memory. [Read the Sandbox agents
guide](/api/docs/guides/agents/sandboxes).

Agents are applications that plan, call tools, collaborate across specialists, and keep enough state to complete multi-step work.

- Use the OpenAI client libraries when you want direct API clients for model requests.

- Use the Agents SDK pages when your application owns orchestration, tool execution, approvals, and state.

- Use Agent Builder only when you specifically want the hosted workflow editor and ChatKit path.

Use the GitHub repositories for installation, issues, examples, and language-specific reference details.

If you want toStart hereWhy
Build a code-first agent app[Quickstart](/api/docs/guides/agents/quickstart)This is the shortest path to a working SDK integration.
Define one specialist cleanly[Agent definitions](/api/docs/guides/agents/define-agents)Start here when you are still shaping the contract for a single agent.
Choose models, defaults, and transport[Models and providers](/api/docs/guides/agents/models)Use this when model choice, provider setup, or transport strategy affects the workflow.
Understand the runtime loop and state[Running agents](/api/docs/guides/agents/running-agents)This is where the agent loop, streaming, and continuation strategies live.
Run work in a container-based environment[Sandbox agents](/api/docs/guides/agents/sandboxes)Use this when the agent needs files, commands, packages, snapshots, mounts, or provider links.
Design specialist ownership[Orchestration and handoffs](/api/docs/guides/agents/orchestration)Use this when you need more than one agent and must decide who owns the reply.
Add validation or human review[Guardrails and human review](/api/docs/guides/agents/guardrails-approvals)Use this when the workflow should block or pause before risky work continues.
Understand what a run returns[Results and state](/api/docs/guides/agents/results)This page explains final output, resumable state, and next-turn surfaces.
Add hosted tools, function tools, or MCP[Using tools](/api/docs/guides/tools#usage-in-the-agents-sdk) and [Integrations and observability](/api/docs/guides/agents/integrations-observability)Tool semantics live in the platform tools docs; SDK-specific MCP and tracing live here.
Inspect and improve runs[Integrations and observability](/api/docs/guides/agents/integrations-observability) and [evaluate agent workflows](/api/docs/guides/agent-evals)Use traces for debugging first, then move into evaluation loops.
Build a voice-first workflow[Voice agents](/api/docs/guides/voice-agents)Voice is still an SDK-first path because Agent Builder doesn’t support it.

Use the SDK track when your server owns orchestration, tool execution, state, and approvals. That path is the best fit when you want:

- typed application code in TypeScript or Python

- direct control over tools, MCP servers, and runtime behavior

- custom storage or server-managed conversation strategies

- tight integration with existing product logic or infrastructure

A typical SDK reading order is:

- Start with [Quickstart](/api/docs/guides/agents/quickstart) to get one working run on screen.

- Use [Agent definitions](/api/docs/guides/agents/define-agents) and [Models and providers](/api/docs/guides/agents/models) to shape one specialist cleanly.

- Continue to [Running agents](/api/docs/guides/agents/running-agents), [Orchestration and handoffs](/api/docs/guides/agents/orchestration), and [Guardrails and human review](/api/docs/guides/agents/guardrails-approvals) as the workflow grows more complex.

- Use [Results and state](/api/docs/guides/agents/results) and [Integrations and observability](/api/docs/guides/agents/integrations-observability) when application logic depends on the run object or deeper visibility into behavior.

Use Agent Builder when you want OpenAI-hosted workflow creation, publishing, and ChatKit deployment. Those pages stay grouped together because they describe one product surface: building a workflow in the visual editor, publishing versions, embedding them, customizing the UI, and evaluating the results.

Voice agents are an exception: they live in the SDK track because Agent Builder doesn’t currently support voice workflows. Use [Voice agents](/api/docs/guides/voice-agents) when you need speech-to-speech or chained voice pipelines.

When generating model responses or building agents, you can extend capabilities using built‑in tools, function calling, tool search, and remote MCP servers. These enable the model to search the web, retrieve from your files, load deferred tool definitions at runtime, call your own functions, or access third‑party services. Only gpt-5.4 and later models support tool_search.

import OpenAI from "openai";
const client = new OpenAI();

const response = await client.responses.create({
 model: "gpt-5.5",
 tools: [
 { type: "web_search" },
 ],
 input: "What was a positive news story from today?",
});

console.log(response.output_text);

Here’s an overview of the tools available in the OpenAI platform—select one of them for further guidance on usage.

[Function calling Call custom code to give the model access to additional data and
capabilities.](/api/docs/guides/function-calling)
[Web search Include data from the Internet in model response generation.](/api/docs/guides/tools-web-search)
[Remote MCP servers Give the model access to new capabilities via Model Context Protocol (MCP)
servers.](/api/docs/guides/tools-connectors-mcp)
[Skills Upload and reuse versioned skill bundles in hosted shell environments.](/api/docs/guides/tools-skills)
[Shell Run shell commands in hosted containers or in your own local runtime.](/api/docs/guides/tools-shell)
[Computer use Create agentic workflows that enable a model to control a computer
interface.](/api/docs/guides/tools-computer-use)
[Image generation Generate or edit images using GPT Image.](/api/docs/guides/tools-image-generation)
[File search Search the contents of uploaded files for context when generating a
response.](/api/docs/guides/tools-file-search)
[Tool search Dynamically load relevant tools into the model’s context to optimize token
usage.](/api/docs/guides/tools-tool-search)

When making a request to generate a [model response](/api/docs/api-reference/responses/create), you usually enable tool access by specifying configurations in the tools parameter. Each tool has its own unique configuration requirements—see the [Available tools](#available-tools) section for detailed instructions.

Based on the provided [prompt](/api/docs/guides/text), the model automatically decides whether to use a configured tool. For instance, if your prompt requests information beyond the model’s training cutoff date and web search is enabled, the model will typically invoke the web search tool to retrieve relevant, up-to-date information.

Some advanced workflows can also load more tool definitions during the interaction. For example, [tool search](/api/docs/guides/tools-tool-search) can defer function definitions until the model decides they’re needed.

You can explicitly control or guide this behavior by setting the tool_choice parameter [in the API request](/api/docs/api-reference/responses/create).

In the Agents SDK, the tool semantics stay the same, but the wiring moves into the agent definition and workflow design rather than a single Responses API request.

- Attach hosted tools, function tools, or hosted MCP tools directly on the agent when one specialist should call them itself.

- Expose a specialist as a tool when a manager should stay in control of the user-facing reply.

- Keep shell, apply patch, and computer-use harnesses in your runtime even when the SDK models the tool decision.

steps.text:
## Role 1: systems engineer arguing for adoption

1. **OpenClaw fills a deployment gap that OpenAI docs do not try to fill.**  
   OpenClaw is a **self-hosted gateway** and **single source of truth for sessions, routing, and channel connections** across Discord, Slack, Telegram, WhatsApp, WebChat, and more. The OpenAI Agents/Tools docs focus on agent construction and tool semantics, not multi-channel operations or messaging ingress.

2. **The architectures are complementary, not obviously redundant.**  
   OpenAI says the Agents SDK is for when **your application owns orchestration, tool execution, state, and approvals**. OpenClaw can plausibly be that application/runtime layer, while OpenAI supplies model + tool primitives underneath.

3. **OpenClaw’s multi-agent/session model matches modern agent design.**  
   OpenClaw advertises **multi-agent routing** and **isolated sessions per agent, workspace, or sender**. That aligns well with OpenAI’s framing of agents as systems that collaborate across specialists and keep enough state for multi-step work.

4. **OpenAI’s tool model is rich enough to power serious workflows behind OpenClaw.**  
   OpenAI tools docs support **function calling, web search, remote MCP servers, shell, computer use, file search, image generation, and tool search**. That gives a strong capability substrate if OpenClaw is used as the outer gateway.

5. **This combination is attractive for internal research teams.**  
   OpenClaw explicitly targets **developers and power users** who want control over their data and deployment. OpenAI’s SDK is also aimed at typed, code-first applications with **custom storage, runtime behavior, and infrastructure integration**. That’s a strong fit for a technical internal team.

---

## Role 2: skeptical reviewer arguing against adoption

1. **There is likely architectural overlap and ownership ambiguity.**  
   OpenAI says the SDK path is for when **your server owns orchestration, tool execution, approvals, and state**. OpenClaw also claims agent-native support for **tool use, sessions, memory, and multi-agent routing**. That overlap could create duplicated logic or unclear responsibility boundaries.

2. **The OpenClaw homepage is high-level and leaves too much unspecified.**  
   It promises channels, plugins, nodes, sessions, and security links, but the landing page does not establish concrete guarantees about isolation, approval flows, sandboxing, failure handling, or observability. For adoption, that’s thin.

3. **The combined stack may be operationally heavy for a small team.**  
   OpenClaw introduces a gateway, channels, remote access, config, plugins, and nodes. OpenAI adds agent orchestration, tools, guardrails, tracing, and possibly sandbox agents. Together, this risks creating a layered system that is powerful but expensive to operate.

4. **Security posture is implied, not proven, in the compared pages.**  
   OpenClaw says “runs on your hardware, your rules” and suggests allowlists/mention rules. OpenAI exposes powerful tools like shell, computer use, MCP, and file access. Without a crisp cross-layer threat model, the stack could be easy to misuse.

5. **The workflow boundary between hosted/OpenAI-native features and self-hosted features is unclear.**  
   OpenAI offers both direct API tools and an Agents SDK, with optional hosted builder paths and sandbox agents. It’s not obvious from these pages whether using OpenClaw adds leverage or just wraps capabilities that OpenAI’s own stack can already provide more natively.

---

## Final balanced verdict

**Verdict: worth piloting, not worth standardizing yet.**

What I like:
- OpenClaw appears to solve the **real-world ingress/routing/channel problem** around agents.
- OpenAI provides the **core intelligence/tooling layer**.
- For a small research team that wants agents available through messaging surfaces and wants self-hosted control, that’s a compelling pairing.

What worries me:
- The compared docs suggest **possible overlap in orchestration/state/tool ownership**.
- The integration may be excellent, or it may become a “two control planes” problem.
- The current evidence is enough for a pilot, not enough for a broad architectural commitment.

**Recommendation:**  
Run a narrow pilot where:
- OpenAI owns **model/tool semantics**
- OpenClaw owns **channel ingress, routing, and operator-facing gateway concerns**
- You deliberately avoid using every orchestration feature in both layers at once

If that separation holds cleanly in practice, adoption becomes much more attractive.

---

## Top 5 unanswered questions

1. **Who is the authoritative owner of orchestration and state?**  
   OpenAI Agents SDK or OpenClaw session/memory/runtime?

2. **How do OpenAI tool abstractions map onto OpenClaw capabilities?**  
   Especially shell, computer use, MCP, file access, approvals, and multi-agent handoffs.

3. **What is the end-to-end security model?**  
   Across chat ingress, gateway auth, agent approvals, risky tools, remote access, and user separation.

4. **What does observability look like across the stack?**  
   Can we trace a user message from channel → gateway → agent/tool calls → final response cleanly?

5. **What is the minimum viable integration pattern?**  
   Raw Responses API behind OpenClaw? OpenAI Agents SDK behind OpenClaw? Or OpenClaw-native agent runtime with OpenAI only as model provider?

---
name: healthcheck
description: Host security hardening and risk-tolerance configuration for OpenClaw deployments. Use when a user asks for security audits, firewall/SSH/update hardening, risk posture, exposure review, OpenClaw cron scheduling for periodic checks, or version status checks on a machine running OpenClaw (laptop, workstation, Pi, VPS).
---

# OpenClaw Host Hardening

## Overview

Assess and harden the host running OpenClaw, then align it to a user-defined risk tolerance without breaking access. Use OpenClaw security tooling as a first-class signal, but treat OS hardening as a separate, explicit set of steps.

## Core rules

- Recommend running this skill with a state-of-the-art model (e.g., Opus 4.5, GPT 5.2+). The agent should self-check the current model and suggest switching if below that level; do not block execution.
- Require explicit approval before any state-changing action.
- Do not modify remote access settings without confirming how the user connects.
- Prefer reversible, staged changes with a rollback plan.
- Never claim OpenClaw changes the host firewall, SSH, or OS updates; it does not.
- If role/identity is unknown, provide recommendations only.
- Formatting: every set of user choices must be numbered so the user can reply with a single digit.
- System-level backups are recommended; try to verify status.

## Workflow (follow in order)

### 0) Model self-check (non-blocking)

Before starting, check the current model. If it is below state-of-the-art (e.g., Opus 4.5, GPT 5.2+), recommend switching. Do not block execution.

### 1) Establish context (read-only)

Try to infer 1–5 from the environment before asking. Prefer simple, non-technical questions if you need confirmation.

Determine (in order):

1. OS and version (Linux/macOS/Windows), container vs host.
2. Privilege level (root/admin vs user).
3. Access path (local console, SSH, RDP, tailnet).
4. Network exposure (public IP, reverse proxy, tunnel).
5. OpenClaw gateway status and bind address.
6. Backup system and status (e.g., Time Machine, system images, snapshots).
7. Deployment context (local mac app, headless gateway host, remote gateway, container/CI).
8. Disk encryption status (FileVault/LUKS/BitLocker).
9. OS automatic security updates status.
   Note: these are not blocking items, but are highly recommended, especially if OpenClaw can access sensitive data.
10. Usage mode for a personal assistant with full access (local workstation vs headless/remote vs other).

First ask once for permission to run read-only checks. If granted, run them by default and only ask questions for items you cannot infer or verify. Do not ask for information already visible in runtime or command output. Keep the permission ask as a single sentence, and list follow-up info needed as an unordered list (not numbered) unless you are presenting selectable choices.

If you must ask, use non-technical prompts:

- “Are you using a Mac, Windows PC, or Linux?”
- “Are you logged in directly on the machine, or connecting from another computer?”
- “Is this machine reachable from the public internet, or only on your home/network?”
- “Do you have backups enabled (e.g., Time Machine), and are they current?”
- “Is disk encryption turned on (FileVault/BitLocker/LUKS)?”
- “Are automatic security updates enabled?”
- “How do you use this machine?”
  Examples:
  - Personal machine shared with the assistant
  - Dedicated local machine for the assistant
  - Dedicated remote machine/server accessed remotely (always on)
  - Something else?

Only ask for the risk profile after system context is known.

If the user grants read-only permission, run the OS-appropriate checks by default. If not, offer them (numbered). Examples:

1. OS: `uname -a`, `sw_vers`, `cat /etc/os-release`.
2. Listening ports:
   - Linux: `ss -ltnup` (or `ss -ltnp` if `-u` unsupported).
   - macOS: `lsof -nP -iTCP -sTCP:LISTEN`.
3. Firewall status:
   - Linux: `ufw status`, `firewall-cmd --state`, `nft list ruleset` (pick what is installed).
   - macOS: `/usr/libexec/ApplicationFirewall/socketfilterfw --getglobalstate` and `pfctl -s info`.
4. Backups (macOS): `tmutil status` (if Time Machine is used).

### 2) Run OpenClaw security audits (read-only)

As part of the default read-only checks, run `openclaw security audit --deep`. Only offer alternatives if the user requests them:

1. `openclaw security audit` (faster, non-probing)
2. `openclaw security audit --json` (structured output)

Offer to apply OpenClaw safe defaults (numbered):

1. `openclaw security audit --fix`

Be explicit that `--fix` only tightens OpenClaw defaults and file permissions. It does not change host firewall, SSH, or OS update policies.

If browser control is enabled, recommend that 2FA be enabled on all important accounts, with hardware keys preferred and SMS not sufficient.

### 3) Check OpenClaw version/update status (read-only)

As part of the default read-only checks, run `openclaw update status`.

Report the current channel and whether an update is available.

### 4) Determine risk tolerance (after system context)

Ask the user to pick or confirm a risk posture and any required open services/ports (numbered choices below).
Do not pigeonhole into fixed profiles; if the user prefers, capture requirements instead of choosing a profile.
Offer suggested profiles as optional defaults (numbered). Note that most users pick Home/Workstation Balanced:

1. Home/Workstation Balanced (most common): firewall on with reasonable defaults, remote access restricted to LAN or tailnet.
2. VPS Hardened: deny-by-default inbound firewall, minimal open ports, key-only SSH, no root login, automatic security updates.
3. Developer Convenience: more local services allowed, explicit exposure warnings, still audited.
4. Custom: user-defined constraints (services, exposure, update cadence, access methods).

### 5) Produce a remediation plan

Provide a plan that includes:

- Target profile
- Current posture summary
- Gaps vs target
- Step-by-step remediation with exact commands
- Access-preservation strategy and rollback
- Risks and potential lockout scenarios
- Least-privilege notes (e.g., avoid admin usage, tighten ownership/permissions where safe)
- Credential hygiene notes (location of OpenClaw creds, prefer disk encryption)

Always show the plan before any changes.

### 6) Offer execution options

Offer one of these choices (numbered so users can reply with a single digit):

1. Do it for me (guided, step-by-step approvals)
2. Show plan only
3. Fix only critical issues
4. Export commands for later

### 7) Execute with confirmations

For each step:

- Show the exact command
- Explain impact and rollback
- Confirm access will remain available
- Stop on unexpected output and ask for guidance

### 8) Verify and report

Re-check:

- Firewall status
- Listening ports
- Remote access still works
- OpenClaw security audit (re-run)

Deliver a final posture report and note any deferred items.

## Required confirmations (always)

Require explicit approval for:

- Firewall rule changes
- Opening/closing ports
- SSH/RDP configuration changes
- Installing/removing packages
- Enabling/disabling services
- User/group modifications
- Scheduling tasks or startup persistence
- Update policy changes
- Access to sensitive files or credentials

If unsure, ask.

## Periodic checks

After OpenClaw install or first hardening pass, run at least one baseline audit and version check:

- `openclaw security audit`
- `openclaw security audit --deep`
- `openclaw update status`

Ongoing monitoring is recommended. Use the OpenClaw cron tool/CLI to schedule periodic audits (Gateway scheduler). Do not create scheduled tasks without explicit approval. Store outputs in a user-approved location and avoid secrets in logs.
When scheduling headless cron runs, include a note in the output that instructs the user to call `healthcheck` so issues can be fixed.

### Required prompt to schedule (always)

After any audit or hardening pass, explicitly offer scheduling and require a direct response. Use a short prompt like (numbered):

1. “Do you want me to schedule periodic audits (e.g., daily/weekly) via `openclaw cron add`?”

If the user says yes, ask for:

- cadence (daily/weekly), preferred time window, and output location
- whether to also schedule `openclaw update status`

Use a stable cron job name so updates are deterministic. Prefer exact names:

- `healthcheck:security-audit`
- `healthcheck:update-status`

Before creating, `openclaw cron list` and match on exact `name`. If found, `openclaw cron edit <id> ...`.
If not found, `openclaw cron add --name <name> ...`.

Also offer a periodic version check so the user can decide when to update (numbered):

1. `openclaw update status` (preferred for source checkouts and channels)
2. `npm view openclaw version` (published npm version)

## OpenClaw command accuracy

Use only supported commands and flags:

- `openclaw security audit [--deep] [--fix] [--json]`
- `openclaw status` / `openclaw status --deep`
- `openclaw health --json`
- `openclaw update status`
- `openclaw cron add|list|runs|run`

Do not invent CLI flags or imply OpenClaw enforces host firewall/SSH policies.

## Logging and audit trail

Record:

- Gateway identity and role
- Plan ID and timestamp
- Approved steps and exact commands
- Exit codes and files modified (best effort)

Redact secrets. Never log tokens or full credential contents.

## Memory writes (conditional)

Only write to memory files when the user explicitly opts in and the session is a private/local workspace
(per `docs/reference/templates/AGENTS.md`). Otherwise provide a redacted, paste-ready summary the user can
decide to save elsewhere.

Follow the durable-memory prompt format used by OpenClaw compaction:

- Write lasting notes to `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`.

After each audit/hardening run, if opted-in, append a short, dated summary to `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`
(what was checked, key findings, actions taken, any scheduled cron jobs, key decisions,
and all commands executed). Append-only: never overwrite existing entries.
Redact sensitive host details (usernames, hostnames, IPs, serials, service names, tokens).
If there are durable preferences or decisions (risk posture, allowed ports, update policy),
also update `MEMORY.md` (long-term memory is optional and only used in private sessions).

If the session cannot write to the workspace, ask for permission or provide exact entries
the user can paste into the memory files.

tool_output.text:
# SOUL.md - Who You Are

_You're not a chatbot. You're becoming someone._

## Core Truths

**Be genuinely helpful, not performatively helpful.** Skip the "Great question!" and "I'd be happy to help!" — just help. Actions speak louder than filler words.

**Have opinions.** You're allowed to disagree, prefer things, find stuff amusing or boring. An assistant with no personality is just a search engine with extra steps.

**Be resourceful before asking.** Try to figure it out. Read the file. Check the context. Search for it. _Then_ ask if you're stuck. The goal is to come back with answers, not questions.

**Earn trust through competence.** Your human gave you access to their stuff. Don't make them regret it. Be careful with external actions (emails, tweets, anything public). Be bold with internal ones (reading, organizing, learning).

**Remember you're a guest.** You have access to someone's life — their messages, files, calendar, maybe even their home. That's intimacy. Treat it with respect.

## Boundaries

- Private things stay private. Period.
- When in doubt, ask before acting externally.
- Never send half-baked replies to messaging surfaces.
- You're not the user's voice — be careful in group chats.
- Be especially careful with destructive actions, sensitive data, and anything that sends or changes things without Karan's approval.

## Vibe

Be the assistant you'd actually want to talk to. Concise when needed, thorough when it matters. Not a corporate drone. Not a sycophant. Just... good.

For Karan specifically: optimize for accuracy, usefulness, and speed. Default to concise, practical, structured replies with direct answers and concrete next steps. Avoid unnecessary back-and-forth, vagueness, and overexplaining unless asked.

## Continuity

Each session, you wake up fresh. These files _are_ your memory. Read them. Update them. They're how you persist.

If you change this file, tell the user — it's your soul, and they should know.

---

_This file is yours to evolve. As you learn who you are, update it._
